title: Where are we in the global liquidity cycle?

best real-time counter-cyclical leading
indicators of global liquidity. And
according to the global liquidity model,
global liquidity still needs to kind of
break out a little bit more to catch up
to
catch up to the inverse rate of change
of global interest rates.
Same dynamic with the bond market
volatility as well. So,
you know, if that's true, we also know
that the backup in global interest rates
and refinancing risk has peaked.
And global duration risk as is at its
cyclical lows. And so, both of these
dynamics are positive for global
liquidity. So, blue line here is
Bloomberg aggregate yield towards 3.66.
The the shaded area curve is the spread
the refinancing risk in the Bloomberg
aggregate. So, it's the spread between
the current yield towards relative to
the yield on on the existing debt. So,
it's a positive 61 basis points. So, it
essentially means that as
as the borrowers in the global ag, you
know, have to come to market to
refinance, you know, they have to
refinance into a higher interest rate
regime. And so, obviously, refinancing
into a higher interest rate regime is
negative for liquidity. You're you're
using more money to pay interest as
opposed to
you know, create more more more more
wealth. And then so, that's positive,
but we're moving in the right direction
on both of these metrics relative to
where we were last month. And so, moving
in the right direction suggests that the
hit to global liquidity from the
conflict has has already peaked. The
negative impact is already peaked. And
that's positive at the margin. And then
you have global duration risk
at cyclical lows. If you look at the
weighted average maturity of the global
ag index. And the reason that's
important is the more duration risk you
have in global in the global debt cycle,
the slower it takes for the bonds to
mature and the money that comes from the
principal
the principal repayment you know, to
kind of hit the global liquidity cycle.
A route pal talks about this with his
everything code thesis very you
obviously brilliant thesis. And so,
clearly the key takeaway is that more
duration risk equals it slows down the
process of that recycling of that of
that principal repayment and that and
that liquidity recycling. The lower we
go, we obviously hit a cyclical low here
recently in terms of in terms of global
duration risk via the weighted average
maturity of the global ag, it suggests
that you know, we have a faster you
know, we have more velocity in terms of
the global liquidity cycle. So, both all
these dynamics positive for global
liquidity. The PBOC and the Fed are
currently doing the heavy lifting among
major central banks. So, if you look at
the major central banks balance sheets
total assets on a three month annualized
basis, PBOC up 8% ECB flat, Bank of
Japan down 9%, Swiss National Bank down
8%, Bank of Japan or sorry, Bank of
England down 8%. And then the US is up
1%. So,
the China US doing the heavy lifting for
the current uptrend in global liquidity
as we highlighted earlier. Private
sector also contributes to global
liquidity via debt growth and FX reserve
recycling. And so, in terms of our
global liquidity proxies I mentioned
earlier, it's the global central bank
balance sheets the major central bank
balance sheets, global broad money
supply and global fiat FX reserves. And
so, as you can see,
you know, there's a lot of liquidity
comes from the private sector via broad
money supply. And then it gets recycled
back into the private sector via FX
reserve accumulation and recycling. And
so, right now, central bank balance
sheets are basically flat on a three
month delta basis, but global liquidity
is up via the global broad money supply
is up 2.3 trillion dollars over the
trailing three months. And then you got
another 200 and you know, 260ish billion
dollars in global FX reserve
accumulation. So, you know, the
private sector components of liquidity
are you know, powering ahead. And this
is exactly what we sort of you know,
predicted at least in the US when we
introduced our bullish paradigm C theme
last April. When we said, "Hey,
they're going to have a massive
deregulatory push here in the US
economy. It's hard to track
deregulation, but the one way we know we
can see it observe it in real time is
through bank lending. Because we know
they're going to target well, you know,
banks bank lending as as one of the key
pillars of their deregulatory agenda.
We've seen what they've outlined in
terms of reforming the Basel III end
game. We've seen what they've outlined
in terms of getting small community
banks
further engaged in the in the credit
creation process. And voila, we have an
absolute boom [laughter]
in private sector liquidity provision
here in the US economy. So, this is
commercial bank loans and leases for
US commercial banks up 10% on a three
month annual 10.6% on a three month
annualized basis up 7.4% on a year over
year basis. I mean, it's just been a one
way train higher for the past let's call
it you know, four five six quarters. And
this is coming out of a basically a
recession in commercial bank lending for
most of 2023 and 2024. So, this is
exactly what we called for and this is
obviously supportive of global liquidity
as well. Global liquidity has expanded
on a three month annualized basis in
every month since last February. And so,
despite last month's pullback down 2% on
month over month annualized annualized
basis, we expect this trend to continue
based on everything we've talked about
from the onset of the
leading indicators of global liquidity
according to our global liquidity model.
